input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin follows the digital‑shadow reference model, establishing an automated one‑way data flow from the physical object to a digital representation that collects, aggregates, and abstracts contextual data traces for a specific purpose. It provides active software components that interpret the incoming data, enabling passive services such as real‑time monitoring of the physical state, historical data visualization, and generation of data‑driven insights. It does not autonomously control the physical system nor push commands back to it.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) tooling: SysML (including internal block diagrams and state‑machine semantics) for requirements, functional and logical architecture; architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML IBDs to capture components, connectors and composition. Behavioural models are refined with MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines and linked to simulation environments like MathWorks Simulink or other domain‑specific simulators. Development code may be expressed in C++ or Java. The methodology follows approaches such as RFLP (Requirements‑Functions‑Logics‑Physics), OOSEM, SPES or SMArDT to manage abstraction layers and stakeholder views.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital shadow receives an automated one‑way data flow from the physical object; data traces are generated continuously as the physical state changes and are streamed to the digital representation in (near) real‑time. Synchronization is event‑driven at the granularity of individual data traces; the documents do not specify explicit frequencies, latency bounds, or batch intervals.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin is orchestrated according to the Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1) and its concrete realization (Fig. 5). The architecture follows a component‑and‑connector style in which the main elements are Purpose, DataTrace, Source, Asset, Processing, and Model. Data acquisition components (e.g., an MQTT client reading from a Siemens SINUMERIK controller) act as Sources that generate DataTraces associated with a specific Asset (e.g., a robot axis). Processing components consume these traces, invoke simulation or data‑calculation Models, and emit new DataTraces, while the ‘originates from’ association preserves transitive traceability. The system is centrally coordinated: the Processing layer schedules model execution and manages the flow of traces, enabling fine‑grained traceability and resource management across the twin’s components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin leverages Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) artifacts spanning multiple abstraction levels. Structural models are expressed in SysML block definition and internal block diagrams and in architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, or MontiArc, defining components, interfaces and connectors. Behavioral aspects are captured with SysML state‑machine diagrams, MontiArc Automaton, or external simulation models such as MathWorks Simulink dynamic models. Implementation code may be supplied in C++ or Java and linked to the higher‑level models. The model repository therefore contains geometric/component definitions, analytical physics‑based models (e.g., Simulink), and data‑driven behavioral models (e.g., state machines), with explicit traceability links between SysML elements and simulation artifacts, enabling consistent transformation from requirements to executable models.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is pursued by coupling white‑box physics‑based Simulink models with black‑box data‑driven machine‑learning models to capture complex, non‑linear phenomena. Validation and verification are centred on traceability of model artifacts and correctness of model transformations, such as ensuring that SysML specifications are accurately converted into simulation models. Uncertainty arising from data‑driven components is addressed through probabilistic latent‑variable modelling. Quality assurance processes include verification of transformation steps, maintenance of traceability links to source data, and continuous comparison of model predictions with observed physical behaviour.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The digital shadow receives an automated one‑way data flow from the physical object, capturing contextual data traces that are aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose. This flow is uni‑directional from the physical state to the digital representation and does not involve feedback to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the Digital Twin follows an MBSE‑driven process that employs SysML for requirements and architecture definition and a range of Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML internal block diagrams to capture components, connectors, and behavior (state machines, automata). The workflow integrates simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) and code (C++, Java) for detailed behavior modeling. A Digital Shadow Reference Model is then concretized: a reference class diagram is instantiated by mapping concrete classes and relations to the abstract concepts, establishing a conformance relation. Processing components consume input data traces, invoke engineering, simulation, or data‑calculation models, and emit derived traces, while maintaining provenance via the “originates‑from” association for full traceability. This method supports iterative versioning of models and evolution of the Twin through re‑instantiation of the reference model and replacement of processing components.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Active components of the Digital Twin ingest incoming data traces, apply the associated engineering or simulation models, and produce new data traces that represent higher‑level abstractions. The system records fine‑grained traceability links that connect each output trace to the processing component, the original input traces, and the specific models employed, enabling users to follow the data lineage for verification or further analysis.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach conforms to established modeling standards: SysML is used for system‑level modelling; ADLs including ACME, AADL, and MontiArc provide standardized component‑connector descriptions; and the Digital Shadow Reference Model employs a formal conformance relation to ensure that concrete realizations adhere to the reference class diagram. These standards facilitate interoperability across tools and stakeholder groups and provide a certified basis for model exchange and integration.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[62] Z. Huang, Y. Shen, J. Li, M. Fey, and C. Brecher, “A survey on aidriven digital twins in industry 4.0: Smart manufacturing and advanced
robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)
[12-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

[66] H. M. Muctadir, D. A. Manrique Negrin, R. Gunasekaran, L. Cleophas,
M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct
[2024. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10270-024-01167-z)

The extraction accurately reflects the digital shadow reference model and specific services described in the text. It correctly details the one-way data flow, the passive nature of the shadow, and the need for active components to interpret data for services like monitoring and visualization.

The extraction is excellent, directly reflecting the detailed tooling overview in Section II.A. It accurately lists the MBSE languages (SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc), simulation tools (MathWorks Simulink), programming languages (C++, Java), and methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) described in the text.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the digital shadow as having an 'automated one-way data flow' and being 'real-time', which aligns with the source text's definition of a digital shadow and mention of 'real-time, data-driven representations'. It correctly notes that specific frequencies and latency bounds are not specified. However, the claim that synchronization is 'event-driven' is an interpretation not explicitly found in the text.

The text focuses on a single digital shadow for a specific robot axis (FirstAxis). It describes components within this single instance and does not mention multiple digital twin instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins. The 'Not in Document' statement is correct.

The extraction is highly detailed and accurate, describing the orchestration based on the 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' and its 'concrete realization'. It correctly lists the main concepts ('Purpose, DataTrace, Source, Asset, Processing, and Model') and accurately describes the data flow: 'Processing component performs these computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and produces output data'. It also correctly notes the role of the 'originates from' association for 'transitive' traceability.

The text describes integration with the robot's controller (Siemens SINUMERIK) and the use of MQTT for data streaming, but these are part of the direct data acquisition loop. It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or broad interoperability standards beyond the immediate context. The 'Not in Document' statement regarding horizontal integration with external systems appears accurate.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the modeling approach discussed in the text, including the use of MBSE, SysML for structure (BDD, IBD), and Simulink for behavior (physics-based friction models). It correctly identifies the types of data (traces, points) and the concept of traceability. However, it lists specific tools (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, C++, Java) that appear only in the general background section (II.A) as examples in the field, rather than tools explicitly used in this specific implementation (which relies on SysML and Simulink). Despite this minor over-generalization from the background section, it captures the core modeling details well. Supported by quotes: 'The Block Definition Diagram (bdd) defines the types of the system', 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink', 'Both the models and the robot generate data that remains fully traceable to its origin'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but Section III explicitly describes the physical actuating components, including the motor and controller, along with their capabilities and constraints. Supported by quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', 'safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but Section III provides explicit details on the sensing infrastructure, including sensor types and their spatial distribution. Supported by quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive', 'The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox'.

The description correctly captures the system's approach to fidelity by coupling white-box and black-box models and emphasizes traceability and comparison with observed data for validation. However, it includes an unsupported specific claim that uncertainty is addressed through 'probabilistic latent-variable modelling', which is not mentioned in the text. The core concepts are supported, but the inclusion of the unsupported methodology reduces the score. Supported quotes: 'combine white- and black-box friction models', 'traceability allows for a meaningful linking of all data', 'compare simulated data versus measured data'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

The description is highly specific and aligns well with the text. It also correctly identifies the processing components and the dynamic exchange of models. The extraction accurately identifies the standards used (SysML, ADLs) and the conformance relation of the Reference Model. It correctly links these to interoperability with tools and stakeholders. ACME, AADL, MontiArc', 'conformance relation that precisely identifies the set of conforming models', 'cope with models in different modeling notations and different tools'.
